subject: Physics
chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Examine the scenario illustrated in the figure. The straight wire is kept fixed, whereas the loop is free to move under the influence of magnetic force. As a result, the loop will
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to the straight wire carrying current $i_1$ is directed into the plane of the paper at the location of the loop.

The magnetic forces on the top and bottom horizontal segments of the loop are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, cancelling each other out.

The left vertical segment carries current in the same direction as the straight wire. Parallel currents attract, so it experiences an attractive force $F_1$ towards the wire.

The right vertical segment carries current in the opposite direction to the straight wire. Antiparallel currents repel, so it experiences a repulsive force $F_2$ away from the wire.

The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the straight wire decreases with distance ($B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i_1}{2\pi r}$).

Since the left segment is closer to the straight wire than the right segment, the magnetic field is stronger at the left segment. Therefore, the attractive force $F_1$ is greater than the repulsive force $F_2$.

The net force on the loop is directed towards the straight wire, causing the loop to move towards it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A straight, current-carrying wire is positioned along the axis of a current-carrying circular loop. The straight wire
options: will exert zero force on the circular loop
will apply an outward force on the circular loop
will apply an inward force on the circular loop
will apply a force on the circular loop parallel to itself
questions.solution:
Let the straight current-carrying wire be placed along the z-axis. The magnetic field produced by this wire at a distance $r$ is given by $\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \hat{\phi}$, which is tangential to any circle centered on the axis.

The circular loop is centered on the axis, so its current elements $d\vec{l}$ are also directed along the tangential direction ($\hat{\phi}$).

The magnetic force on a small current element of the loop is given by $d\vec{F} = I (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$.

Since both the current element $d\vec{l}$ and the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ are along the same line (parallel or anti-parallel), the cross product $d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}$ is zero.

Therefore, the straight wire exerts zero magnetic force on the circular loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A particle is projected into a plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field. The area enclosed by the path traced by the particle is proportional to
questions.solution:
When a charged particle is projected perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, it moves in a circular path of radius $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$.

The area enclosed by this circular path is $A = \pi r^2 = \pi \left( \dfrac{mv}{qB} \right)^2 = \dfrac{\pi m^2 v^2}{q^2 B^2}$.

The kinetic energy of the particle is $K = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$, which gives $m^2 v^2 = 2mK$.

Substituting this in the expression for area, we get:

$A = \dfrac{2\pi m K}{q^2 B^2}$

Since $m$, $q$, and $B$ are constants, $A \propto K$.

Thus, the area enclosed by the path is directly proportional to the kinetic energy of the particle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A proton beam travels from north to south, and an electron beam travels from south to north. Neglecting the magnetic field of the earth, the electron beam will be deflected
questions.solution:
The direction of electric current is taken as the direction of flow of positive charge.

The proton beam travels from north to south, so the current due to it is from north to south.

The electron beam travels from south to north. Since electrons are negatively charged, the current due to the electron beam is opposite to its direction of motion, which is also from north to south.

Both beams represent parallel currents flowing in the same direction.

Since parallel currents in the same direction attract each other, the electron beam will be deflected towards the proton beam.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A vertical wire carries an upward current. An electron beam travelling horizontally towards the wire will be deflected
questions.solution:
Let the vertical wire be placed along the y-axis with the current $I$ flowing in the $+\hat{j}$ direction (upwards).

Let the electron beam travel horizontally along the x-axis towards the wire. Its velocity vector is $\vec{v} = -v \hat{i}$.

According to the right-hand thumb rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by the current-carrying wire at a point on the positive x-axis is directed outwards, i.e., in the $+\hat{k}$ direction.

The magnetic force acting on the moving electron is given by the Lorentz force formula:

$\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$

Substituting the charge of an electron $q = -e$ and the respective vectors:

$\vec{F} = -e(-v \hat{i} \times B \hat{k})$

$\vec{F} = e v B (\hat{i} \times \hat{k})$

Since $\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$, we get:

$\vec{F} = -e v B \hat{j}$

The force is in the $-\hat{j}$ direction, which corresponds to the downward direction. Therefore, the electron beam will be deflected downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two particles $X$ and $Y$ carrying the same charge are accelerated through an identical potential difference before entering a region with a uniform magnetic field. They describe circular paths of radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ respectively. The ratio of the mass of $X$ to the mass of $Y$ is
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy gained by a particle of charge $q$ accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is $K = qV$.

The momentum of the particle is $p = \sqrt{2mK} = \sqrt{2mqV}$.

When the particle enters a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicularly, the radius of its circular path is given by $R = \dfrac{p}{qB} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2mqV}}{qB}$.

Squaring both sides, we get $R^2 = \dfrac{2mV}{qB^2}$, which gives $m = \dfrac{qB^2 R^2}{2V}$.

Since $q$, $V$, and $B$ are the same for both particles $X$ and $Y$, the mass is directly proportional to the square of the radius, $m \propto R^2$.

Therefore, the ratio of their masses is $\dfrac{m_X}{m_Y} = \left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire with a cross-sectional area $A$ carries a current $i$. Suppose the number of free electrons per unit volume is $n$. An observer is positioned on a trolley that travels in the direction opposite to the current at a speed $v = \dfrac{i}{nAe}$, keeping a separation distance $r$ from the wire. The magnetic field observed by this person is approximately
questions.solution:
The drift velocity of free electrons in the wire is given by $v_d = \dfrac{i}{nAe}$.

The direction of the drift velocity of electrons is opposite to the direction of the conventional current.

The trolley moves in the direction opposite to the current with speed $v = \dfrac{i}{nAe}$, which is exactly equal to the drift velocity of the electrons.

In the reference frame of the trolley, the free electrons are at rest, so they do not contribute to the current.

However, the positive ions of the metallic lattice, which are stationary in the ground frame, appear to move with velocity $v$ in the direction of the current.

The linear charge density of the positive ions is $\lambda_+ = nAe$.

The current due to the apparent motion of positive ions in the trolley frame is $i' = \lambda_+ v = (nAe) \left(\dfrac{i}{nAe}\right) = i$.

Since the net current in the trolley frame remains $i$, the magnetic field observed by the person is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two long, parallel wires carry currents $i_1$ and $i_2$ such that $i_1 > i_2$. The magnetic field at a midway point between the wires is $10 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ when the currents travel in the same direction. If the direction of $i_2$ is reversed, the magnetic field becomes $30 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. Determine the ratio $i_1/i_2$.
questions.solution:
Let the distance between the wires be $2d$. The magnetic field at the midway point due to a long straight wire carrying current $i$ is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi d}$.

When the currents are in the same direction, the magnetic fields at the midway point oppose each other. Since $i_1 > i_2$, the net magnetic field is:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi d}(i_1 - i_2) = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

When the direction of $i_2$ is reversed, the currents are in opposite directions, and their magnetic fields at the midway point add up:

$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi d}(i_1 + i_2) = 30 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

Dividing the two equations, we get:

$\dfrac{i_1 + i_2}{i_1 - i_2} = \dfrac{30}{10} = 3$

$i_1 + i_2 = 3i_1 - 3i_2$

$2i_1 = 4i_2$

$\dfrac{i_1}{i_2} = 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two parallel wires carry currents of $20 \text{ A}$ and $40 \text{ A}$ in opposite directions. A third wire, which carries a current antiparallel to the $20 \text{ A}$ current, is positioned exactly midway between the first two wires. The magnetic force acting on this third wire will be directed
options: perpendicular to the plane of the currents
questions.solution:
Let the first wire carry a current $I_1 = 20 \text{ A}$ in the upward direction and the second wire carry $I_2 = 40 \text{ A}$ in the downward direction.

The third wire is placed midway between them and carries a current $I_3$ antiparallel to $I_1$, which means $I_3$ is in the downward direction.

The magnetic force between two parallel current-carrying wires is attractive if the currents are in the same direction, and repulsive if they are in opposite directions.

The current in the third wire ($I_3$) and the first wire ($I_1$) are in opposite directions. Therefore, the first wire repels the third wire, exerting a force directed away from the $20 \text{ A}$ wire (towards the $40 \text{ A}$ wire).

The current in the third wire ($I_3$) and the second wire ($I_2$) are in the same direction (both downward). Therefore, the second wire attracts the third wire, exerting a force directed towards the $40 \text{ A}$ wire.

Since both individual forces acting on the third wire are directed towards the $40 \text{ A}$ wire, the net magnetic force on it will also be directed towards the $40 \text{ A}$ wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular loop is placed in a vertical plane containing the north-south direction. The current it carries flows towards the north at the loop's topmost point. Suppose $A$ is a point on the circle's axis located to the east of it, and $B$ is a point on the same axis located to the west. The magnetic field generated by this loop
options: points towards the west at $A$ and towards the east at $B$
points towards the east at $A$ and towards the west at $B$
points towards the east at both $A$ and $B$
points towards the west at both $A$ and $B$
questions.solution:
Let the East direction be along the $+x$-axis, the North direction be along the $+y$-axis, and the vertically upward direction be along the $+z$-axis.

The circular loop is placed in the North-South vertical plane, which corresponds to the $y$-$z$ plane. The axis of the loop lies along the East-West direction, which is the $x$-axis.

At the topmost point of the loop, the position vector with respect to the center is $\vec{r} = R\hat{k}$.

The current at this topmost point flows towards the North, so the current element vector is $d\vec{l} = dl\hat{j}$.

According to the Biot-Savart law, the direction of the magnetic field at the center of the loop is given by the direction of $d\vec{l} \times (-\vec{r})$.

Calculating the cross product:
$\hat{j} \times (-\hat{k}) = -(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) = -\hat{i}$

The $-\hat{i}$ direction corresponds to the West. Thus, the magnetic dipole moment of the loop points towards the West.

For a circular current-carrying loop, the magnetic field along its entire central axis points in the same direction as the magnetic dipole moment.

Therefore, the magnetic field points towards the West at all points on the axis, including both point $A$ (to the East) and point $B$ (to the West).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A charged particle travels along a magnetic field line. The magnetic force acting on the particle is
questions.solution:
The magnetic force acting on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

The magnitude of this force is $F = qvB\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ and the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$.

Since the particle is traveling along a magnetic field line, its velocity is either parallel ($\theta = 0^{\circ}$) or anti-parallel ($\theta = 180^{\circ}$) to the magnetic field.

In both cases, $\sin\theta = 0$.

Therefore, the magnetic force acting on the particle is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: When a charge is in motion, it generates
options: neither an electric nor a magnetic field
questions.solution:
A stationary electric charge produces only an electric field in its surrounding space.

When the charge is in motion, it constitutes an electric current. According to electromagnetism, a moving charge produces both an electric field and a magnetic field.
